_comment: Base pace ANCHORED to real 2026 Silverstone data (see _calibration below); tyre-deg and overtaking values remain hand-tuned placeholders pending FastF1 (via /calibrate/calibrate.py). v1 keeps pace FLATTENED: every car uses this single shared model (spec sections 1-2). Do not present the deg/overtaking numbers as measured F1 figures yet.

_calibration.note: Shared base lap calibrated from real data per spec section 4 'calibrate base only' decision. Per-car pace intentionally NOT modelled in v1 (flattened).
_calibration.reasoning: base_lap_seconds 91.0 = pole 88.376 + ~2.6s realistic race-fuel/management offset, and sits safely above the 84.892s low-fuel fastest-lap floor. pit_loss 20.5s matches Silverstone's ~20-21s pit time loss.

_pit_realism_note: Extra cost per stop beyond the static pit-lane time-loss: cold-tyre out-lap (real F1 out-laps run measurably slower for 1-2 laps until temperature builds) plus rejoin/traffic risk. Without this, degradation time is convex in stint length, so an EXACT optimiser (src/dp.js) mathematically always prefers more/shorter stints even when real strategists don't, because the model had nothing else discouraging an extra stop. NOT a cited figure — calibrated by sweeping 0/8/12/18/20/24/30 against the R1-8 backtest and picking the value (20) that restores winner/podium accuracy to the pre-change baseline (57% winner, 1.6/3 podium) while most improving stop-count plausibility (R8 review: 5/22 -> 12/22 agreement). Residual gap vs the old hand-tuned GA's 17/22 is an honest open item — see progress.md.

tyre_allocation._comment: Standard 2026 dry-race slick allocation per driver per race weekend (practice+quali+race combined): 2 hard, 3 medium, 8 soft sets (13 total; sprint weekends get 12 with a different split — not modelled). We treat this as a race-strategy cap on how many DIFFERENT stints of a compound the race plan may use (each stint assumed to need one set; conservative since real teams can reuse a scrubbed set for a 2nd short stint, which we don't model). Intermediate/wet have a much larger wet-weather allocation, so no binding cap here.
tyre_allocation._source: Pirelli official 2026 nomination releases (press.pirelli.com), e.g. the Austrian GP release: 'two sets of the hard tyre, three sets of the medium tyre, and eight sets of the soft tyre'.

compound_model._comment: Absolute Pirelli slick compounds, C1 (hardest) through C5 (softest), 2026 range (no C6). pace_offset is seconds slower than the softest (C5) at reference fuel. deg_per_lap is per-lap time loss growth on a severity 1.0 track. Both are monotonic in hardness. Per race, data/races.json maps hard/medium/soft to a C-number, and strategy_lib re-centres pace on that track's medium and scales deg by tyre_severity, so a soft at Silverstone (C3) is correctly treated as a durable tyre and a soft at Austria (C5) as a fast-degrading one. This is what makes the compound recommendation, not just the stop count, track-accurate.
compound_model._source: Pirelli C1-C5 characteristics, deg anchored to 2026 FastF1 race data and held monotonic (raw measured deg is too noisy to order compounds cleanly, see the strategy_lib note). pace_offset gaps were recalibrated after we noticed the model almost never recommended the hard compound (used in only 19% of predictions vs constantly in real races): the original uniform 0.45s step made a harder tyre's fixed pace penalty outweigh its lower deg almost everywhere, since breakeven only arrived past 30+ lap stints that our own cliff limits rarely allow. Halving the steps (uniform 0.225s now) fixed this: compound-choice agreement against real 2026 strategies went from 23/134 (17%) to 81/134 (60%), and stop-count agreement improved too (66/134 to 77/134), with no change to the separate real-strategy pace backtest (rho 0.62, unaffected since that replay doesn't touch this constant). Not independently re-sourced per compound step, just fixed against the data we have.
compound_model._max_stint_laps_caveat: Honesty note: there is no official blanket FIA or Pirelli maximum laps per compound rule for standard races. The one real precedent, a mandatory 25 lap per set limit, was a track-specific emergency mandate for the abrasive Qatar surface in 2025, not a universal rule. These max_stint_laps figures are our sourced technical estimate of where a stint becomes unraceable (the cliff), anchored to Pirelli's own published characterisation: softest/red compounds show noticeable degradation within 6 to 8 laps and are rarely pushed much beyond that before falling off a cliff, while hardest/white compounds comfortably run 20 to 40+ laps. See redbull.com, 'Formula 1 tires: everything you need to know', and Pirelli's C1-C5 technical briefings. Treat as a credible modelling constraint, not a cited rule.

overtake._comment: Overtaking model v2 (spec section 5), recalibrated for real per-car pace. Pass probability per lap = prob_cap * (1 - exp(-pass_k * (advantage - pace_gap_threshold))) where advantage = rolling-pace edge (s/lap) over the car directly ahead, attempted only within dirty_air_window seconds. Saturating: a few tenths stays stuck in dirty air, 1s+/lap passes within a lap or two, lapping-speed gaps are near-certain. dirty_air: seconds added when within the window and no pass this lap.

safety_car._comment: Safety-car model (spec section 5). Each green lap (until last_deploy_laps_left from the end, one deployment max) an SC deploys with prob_per_lap (~0.018 => ~60% of races see one, matching real SC/VSC frequency). Under SC: everyone laps at base*pace_multiplier (gaps freeze), no overtaking, tyres age at half rate, pit loss * pit_loss_multiplier (the cheap stop). Restart bunches the field to bunch_gap_s, order preserved. Rivals with stops in hand and tyre age >= rival_pit_min_age take the cheap stop; fixed plans shift their next stop onto the SC if due within fixed_shift_window laps.
